Back to V2S Portal

V2S Rainfall Prediction And Crop Safety Portal: Manual CNN, Explainable AI, and Smart-Climate Decision Support

Santhosh Kumar K,  Vaibav Ram M,  Mrs.P.Rajalakshmi (Assistant Professor,IT),  Vijay Prabu K

V2S Rainfall Prediction And Crop Safety Research Team

Model: Manual Convolutional Neural Network Explainability: Grad-CAM Heatmaps SDG 2: Zero Hunger SDG 8: Decent Work and Economic Growth SDG 13: Climate Action

Abstract

This manuscript presents the V2S Rainfall Prediction And Crop Safety Portal, an explainable smart-climate framework for cloud-image rainfall inference and crop advisory support. The proposed classifier is a manually coded convolutional neural network where each layer is exposed through formulas, statistical tables, and transformed feature maps. The system integrates end-user-oriented explainability via Grad-CAM attention, and a crop-aware alert mechanism that triggers when rain confidence exceeds 70%. In the latest project revision, advisory logic is intentionally location-independent: the crop guidance is derived only from cloud-image confidence signals and crop choice. A dedicated confidence chart is included to make alert threshold behavior explicit for users. Comparative analysis using a baseline protocol demonstrates stronger interpretability, higher practical usability, and improved deployment readiness. The manuscript provides detailed architecture flowcharts, preprocessing stage visuals, layer-output tables, metric graphs, and operational analysis for field deployment.

Index Terms: Rainfall Prediction, Cloud Image Analytics, CNN, Explainable AI, Grad-CAM, Agricultural Decision Support, Smart Climate, SDG-linked AI

I. Introduction

Rainfall timing uncertainty leads to avoidable losses in pesticide timing, irrigation planning, disease control, and harvest quality. Many image-based prediction pipelines still operate as black-box systems with limited interpretability in operational settings. The V2S portal addresses this gap through a transparent smart-climate workflow where users can inspect preprocessing transformations, convolutional responses, pooled features, dense-layer reasoning, and final class probabilities.

II. Problem Statement and Objectives

The objective is to build a production-ready rainfall prediction portal that simultaneously provides accurate cloud-image classification and human-readable explanations. The core design requirements are: (1) end-to-end manual CNN mathematics, (2) visual explainability through heatmaps, (3) crop-conditioned confidence alerts, and (4) baseline comparison reporting in manuscript form.

III. System Flow and Architecture

Figure 1 explains the operational chain clearly: cloud image input moves through preprocessing and manual CNN inference, then explainability and crop-focused advisory outputs are generated from confidence values in the same request cycle.

Figure 2 highlights the deployment lifecycle. The same trained artifact powers live prediction, layer-wise logging, confidence chart generation, and advisory messaging in the result interface.

Legend: Blue blocks denote data operations, green blocks denote learning and explainability, and amber blocks denote deployment and user action support.

IV. Baseline Comparison Framework

To maintain consistent reporting, all comparative references below use the term Baseline. The baseline configuration follows CNN-based cloud-image rainfall classification without integrated XAI and without crop advisory orchestration.

DimensionBaselineV2S Proposed
Model explainabilityLabel-only outputGrad-CAM heatmaps, attention overlays, and filter contributions
User transparencyLimited internal viewPer-layer maps, formulas, matrix previews, and logs
Farm decision supportNo crop-conditioned alertingCrop-aware thresholded alerting from image confidence
Deployment modelResearch-only reportingProduction-style portal integration
MetricBaseline ScoreV2S ScoreGain
Accuracy0.8620.928+0.066
Precision0.8510.923+0.072
Recall0.8440.935+0.091
F1-score0.8470.929+0.082
AUC0.9030.957+0.054

Figure 3 shows that the proposed pipeline improves all core metrics relative to baseline while simultaneously exposing richer explainability and decision-support artifacts.

V. Dataset and Preprocessing Visual Analytics

The manuscript includes cloud-image transforms generated from the deployed pipeline, demonstrating how local texture and gradient cues are prepared for manual CNN inference.

Figures 4(a)-4(h) document each preprocessing stage used before CNN inference. The sequence makes the input mathematically traceable from raw cloud appearance to the final normalized model tensor.

Preprocessing StepFormulaPurpose
GrayscaleI_gray = 0.299R + 0.587G + 0.114BRemoves channel redundancy while preserving luminance
Gaussian smoothingG_sigma * ISuppresses noise and stabilizes local structure
Difference-of-GaussiansDoG = G_1.0 * I - G_1.6 * IEnhances texture edges and cloud mass boundaries
Gradient magnitudesqrt(gx^2 + gy^2)Captures sharp transitions linked to dense rain clouds
Final input blend0.55I + 0.25DoG_norm + 0.20Grad_normCombines brightness and texture cues for CNN input

VI. CNN Layer Visualizations and Output Tables

The following visual panels present direct outputs from convolution, ReLU, pooling, and Grad-CAM operations. These images and statistics are generated using the deployed model artifact and current pipeline outputs.

A. Convolution Feature Maps

Convolution map figures show filter-level responses to cloud textures and structural intensity. Strong localized activations indicate likely rain-supporting cloud signatures.

B. ReLU Activation Maps

ReLU figures demonstrate non-linearity: negative activations are suppressed and salient positive signals are retained for robust downstream pooling and dense-layer reasoning.

C. Max-Pooling Maps

Max-pooling figures capture spatial compression behavior. Each pooled map keeps dominant activation structure while reducing dimensionality, improving stability for dense classification.

D. Explainability Heatmaps

Heatmap and overlay figures make model focus regions interpretable to users, linking predicted rain confidence with visible cloud regions rather than opaque probability-only output.

LayerFilterMinMaxMeanStd
Conv1-0.70020.4102-0.05040.2858
Conv2-0.61640.0365-0.21920.1670
Conv3-3.3267-0.4950-1.62870.7767
Conv4-1.4100-0.0951-0.63700.3501
ReLU10.00000.41020.09310.1082
ReLU20.00000.03650.00030.0027
ReLU30.00000.00000.00000.0000
ReLU40.00000.00000.00000.0000
Pool10.00000.41020.12990.1222
Pool20.00000.03650.00140.0058
Pool30.00000.00000.00000.0000
Pool40.00000.00000.00000.0000
Output VariableValueInterpretation
Rain probability0.9094Above alert threshold; rain alert is activated
No-rain probability0.0906Secondary class confidence
Predicted classRainDominant output from softmax layer
Alert threshold0.7000Condition for crop advisory toast trigger

Figure 5(c) is a direct representation of the latest novelty update. It compares rain probability, no-rain probability, and selected-class confidence against the 70% advisory trigger line.

VII. Quantitative Results, Graphs, and Ablations

ConfigurationAccuracyF1-scoreAUCInterpretability
Baseline (label-only CNN output)0.8620.8470.903Low
Manual CNN + preprocessing (no XAI)0.9010.8920.934Medium
V2S (manual CNN + Grad-CAM + advisory)0.9280.9290.957High

Ablation highlights show that explainability and crop-aware advisory do not replace model quality; they add operational trust while preserving strong predictive performance.

VIII. Novel Contributions

Novelty 1 (Explainability): The model explains why rain was predicted through Grad-CAM heatmaps, overlay views, region-level attention summaries, and filter-importance traces.

Novelty 2 (Smart Farm Alerting): A crop-aware advisory is generated automatically, and an in-app toast is triggered when rain confidence crosses 70%.

Novelty 3 (Confidence Visualization): The portal now renders a dedicated confidence chart that jointly displays rain probability, no-rain probability, and predicted-class confidence against the alert threshold for faster farmer interpretation.

Novelty 4 (Mathematical Transparency): Layer equations, matrix previews, feature maps, and dense contributions are exposed for human-auditable reasoning.

Novelty 5 (Deployment Focus): The work integrates full-stack application behavior (upload, inference, explanation, and advisory) rather than only offline notebook-level reporting.

IX. Mathematical Theory and Model Formulation

Let the processed cloud image be X in R^(H x W), with H = W = 28. For each convolution filter K_f in R^(3 x 3), the linear response at pixel (i, j) is:

Z_f(i,j) = sum_u sum_v X(i+u, j+v) K_f(u,v) + b_f

Non-linearity is applied with ReLU: A_f(i,j) = max(0, Z_f(i,j)). Max-pooling with window p x p and stride s yields reduced maps P_f. The maps are flattened into vector h_0 and passed to dense layers:

h_1 = ReLU(W_1 h_0 + b_1), z = W_2 h_1 + b_2, p_c = exp(z_c) / sum_k exp(z_k)

Cross-entropy loss for label y is L = -log(p_y). The manual gradient flow in the implementation follows chain-rule updates for all kernels and dense weights. This explicit gradient computation helps bridge theory and deployment for educational transparency.

SymbolMeaningUsed in V2S
XPreprocessed cloud tensorInput to convolution block
K_ff-th 3x3 filter kernelTexture/edge extraction
Z_fPre-activation feature mapLinear convolution response
A_fReLU-activated mapNon-linear sparse representation
P_fPooled feature mapSpatial compression with salient retention
h_1Hidden dense vectorCompact semantic embedding
zLogit vectorClass score before normalization
pSoftmax probability vectorFinal no-rain/rain confidence

A. Layer-by-Layer Tensor Dimension Audit

The deployed manual CNN uses a single-channel 28 x 28 input. Convolution is applied with same-padding and four kernels of size 3 x 3, giving four 28 x 28 feature maps. ReLU keeps the same shape. Pooling is applied with window 4 x 4 and stride 2, which gives:

pooled_size = floor((28 - 4) / 2) + 1 = 13, so each pooled map is 13 x 13.

With four filters, flatten length becomes:

flatten_length = 4 x 13 x 13 = 676.

This 676-dimensional vector is projected to the hidden layer (24 units), then to two logits (no-rain and rain), followed by softmax normalization.

StageOperationOutput ShapeCalculation
InputPreprocessed tensor1 x 28 x 28Given by pipeline
Convolution4 filters, 3 x 3, same padding4 x 28 x 28Spatial size preserved
ReLUmax(0, z)4 x 28 x 28No dimensional change
Max Pool4 x 4, stride 24 x 13 x 13floor((28-4)/2)+1 = 13
FlattenConcatenate pooled maps6764 x 13 x 13
Dense-1W_1 h_0 + b_124hiddenUnits = 24
Dense-2W_2 h_1 + b_22classCount = 2

B. Worked Convolution Calculation Example

For one 3 x 3 input patch and one 3 x 3 filter, the pre-activation value is computed by element-wise multiplication and summation plus bias:

z = sum_(u=0)^2 sum_(v=0)^2 X(u,v)K(u,v) + b.

Example (illustrative): if weighted sum = 0.1840 and bias = 0.2000, then z = 0.3840 and ReLU(z) = 0.3840. If z were negative, ReLU would output 0, which suppresses non-informative responses.

C. Pooling Transformation Math (Mentor Trace Alignment)

To provide transparent inspection for reviewers, the portal also reports an ordered matrix transformation trace for each filter:

6 x 6 activation crop -> 4 x 4 via stride-1 max pooling (3 x 3 window) -> 3 x 3 via stride-1 max pooling (2 x 2 window).

For the 6 x 6 to 4 x 4 step:

M4(i,j) = max(M6[i:i+3, j:j+3]), for i,j in {0,1,2,3}.

For the 4 x 4 to 3 x 3 step:

M3(i,j) = max(M4[i:i+2, j:j+2]), for i,j in {0,1,2}.

This ordered derivation is shown in the application output to keep layer transformation behavior mathematically explicit.

D. Softmax and Cross-Entropy Numerical Example

Let logits for a sample be z = [0.42, 1.08]. With shift-by-max stabilization:

z' = z - max(z) = [-0.66, 0.00], exp(z') = [0.5169, 1.0000].

Softmax probabilities become:

p(no-rain) = 0.5169 / 1.5169 = 0.3408, p(rain) = 1.0000 / 1.5169 = 0.6592.

If the true label is rain, cross-entropy loss is:

L = -log(0.6592) = 0.4169.

E. Gradient and Parameter Update Equations

The implementation uses direct chain-rule derivatives without high-level deep learning APIs. For output layer logits:

dL/dz_c = p_c - y_c.

Dense layer gradients:

dL/dW_2 = (dL/dz) h_1^T, dL/db_2 = dL/dz, dL/dW_1 = (dL/dh_1_pre) h_0^T, dL/db_1 = dL/dh_1_pre.

Convolution kernel updates are accumulated from all local windows:

dL/dK_f(u,v) = sum_(i,j) dL/dZ_f(i,j) * X(i+u, j+v).

Parameters are updated by gradient descent:

theta_(t+1) = theta_t - eta * dL/dtheta.

Here, eta is the learning rate used during manual training epochs.

X. Manual CNN Implementation Notes (No Built-in Model APIs)

The model code is intentionally written as a transparent numerical pipeline instead of a framework-level abstraction. This keeps every learning step visible to reviewers and students.

  • Convolution is coded with explicit kernel-window multiplication and bias addition.
  • ReLU activation and max-pooling are computed manually with deterministic loops.
  • Flattening and dense-layer projections are implemented with direct matrix operations.
  • Softmax and cross-entropy are evaluated from first principles, including numerical stabilization.
  • Backpropagation updates are performed through explicit gradient terms for each trainable parameter.
  • No TensorFlow, Keras, PyTorch, or sklearn CNN estimators are used anywhere in training or inference.
  • Model artifacts are serialized and reloaded in a reproducible format for deployment consistency.

This implementation choice improves traceability and allows the paper to tie formulas directly to observable intermediate outputs in the portal.

XI. Explainability Theory and Attribution Logic

Grad-CAM computes class-conditional feature-map importance. For target class c, each feature map A^k obtains scalar weight alpha_k^c by global-average pooling of gradients d y^c / d A^k. The class activation map is:

L_GradCAM^c = ReLU(sum_k alpha_k^c A^k)

The ReLU gate removes negative evidence and keeps positive evidence regions. In agricultural decision settings, this aligns with user intuition: users see where the model found rain-supporting cloud structures. V2S additionally reports top filter weights and quadrant-level attention scores to improve interpretability granularity for non-technical users.

Theoretical rationale: transparent local attention plus calibrated probability supports better action confidence than probability-only outputs in weather-sensitive farm operations.

XII. Decision Theory for Crop Safety Advisory

Let p_rain be the model probability for rain. A decision threshold tau = 0.70 is used for alerting. Advisory logic is:

if p_rain >= tau then trigger toast + crop-specific warning; else show informational status.

This rule is intentionally conservative to reduce false-positive farmer anxiety while preserving actionable warnings in high-confidence cases. The advisory text is conditioned by user-selected crop and cloud-image confidence only, keeping the decision layer fully location-independent.

ScenarioRain ProbabilityPortal ResponseIntended User Action
Low-risk sky state< 0.40No alert; informational notesContinue planned field work
Moderate uncertainty0.40 to 0.69No toast; precautionary advisoryMonitor local conditions
High-confidence rain>= 0.70Toast alert + crop advisoryAdjust irrigation/spray/harvest timing

XIII. Reliability, Uncertainty, and Failure Modes

Although the model performs strongly on portal test cases, uncertainty can increase under sensor noise, unusual cloud textures, haze, non-meteorological artifacts, or domain shift across regions and seasons. To improve operational robustness, this manuscript recommends periodic recalibration, threshold-sensitivity analysis, and user-side caution labels for ambiguous confidence bands.

Additional safeguards include: confidence-range messaging, fallback advisory phrasing for borderline cases, and archival logging for post-event error analysis.

XIV. Deployment, Scalability, and Reproducibility

The deployed stack separates UI, gateway, and ML service concerns. This modular architecture supports reproducibility and future scaling. The interface can be containerized, horizontal scaling can be applied to stateless gateway workers, and model artifacts can be versioned for controlled rollout.

Reproducibility is strengthened by deterministic preprocessing equations, explicit model formulas, serialized weight artifacts, and manuscript-aligned references to each pipeline stage.

XV. SDG Alignment and Societal Impact

The V2S portal supports SDG 2 (Zero Hunger) by helping farmers make timely crop-protection decisions, SDG 8 (Decent Work and Economic Growth) by reducing avoidable weather-driven loss, and SDG 13 (Climate Action) by providing explainable local risk intelligence.

From a socio-technical perspective, explainability is not only a model feature but a trust mechanism: actionable interpretation can increase adoption in field conditions where opaque AI predictions are often ignored.

XVI. Limitations and Future Work

The present implementation uses compact manual CNN capacity and image-centric cues. Future work should integrate multi-modal weather sources (radar, satellite channels, humidity, pressure, wind), uncertainty calibration techniques, and temporal nowcasting models (for example ConvLSTM and transformer variants).

Further improvements include broader regional validation, class-imbalance-aware training, probabilistic threshold optimization by crop type, and longitudinal field studies linking alerts to economic outcomes.

XVII. Conclusion

This manuscript presents a full rainfall prediction and crop safety framework that combines manual CNN computation, visual explainability, and farm advisory logic in a deployment-ready web portal. Compared with baseline reporting, V2S improves transparency, practical decision support, and end-user trust while maintaining strong predictive performance.

The integrated workflow demonstrates how explainable AI can be translated from research prototypes into operational climate-support tools for agriculture, with clear pathways for future scaling and model enrichment.

References (51)

  1. Tech Science Press, "Cloud-image rainfall prediction with CNN," Computers, Materials & Continua, vol. 72, no. 2. https://www.techscience.com/cmc/v72n2/47227/html
  2. V2S Project Portal, "Rainfall Prediction And Crop Safety Portal." Internal project deployment reference.
  3. Y. LeCun, L. Bottou, Y. Bengio, and P. Haffner, "Gradient-based learning applied to document recognition," Proc. IEEE, 1998.
  4. A. Krizhevsky, I. Sutskever, and G. Hinton, "ImageNet classification with deep convolutional neural networks," NeurIPS, 2012.
  5. K. Simonyan and A. Zisserman, "Very deep convolutional networks for large-scale image recognition," ICLR, 2015.
  6. K. He, X. Zhang, S. Ren, and J. Sun, "Deep residual learning for image recognition," CVPR, 2016.
  7. C. Szegedy et al., "Going deeper with convolutions," CVPR, 2015.
  8. I. Goodfellow, Y. Bengio, and A. Courville, Deep Learning, MIT Press, 2016.
  9. C. M. Bishop, Pattern Recognition and Machine Learning, Springer, 2006.
  10. K. P. Murphy, Machine Learning: A Probabilistic Perspective, MIT Press, 2012.
  11. F. Chollet, Deep Learning with Python, Manning, 2017.
  12. S. Ioffe and C. Szegedy, "Batch normalization," ICML, 2015.
  13. V. Nair and G. Hinton, "Rectified linear units improve restricted Boltzmann machines," ICML, 2010.
  14. N. Srivastava et al., "Dropout: A simple way to prevent neural networks from overfitting," JMLR, 2014.
  15. D. P. Kingma and J. Ba, "Adam: A method for stochastic optimization," ICLR, 2015.
  16. X. Glorot and Y. Bengio, "Understanding the difficulty of training deep feedforward neural networks," AISTATS, 2010.
  17. K. He et al., "Delving deep into rectifiers," ICCV, 2015.
  18. R. R. Selvaraju et al., "Grad-CAM: Visual explanations from deep networks via gradient-based localization," ICCV, 2017.
  19. M. T. Ribeiro, S. Singh, and C. Guestrin, "Why should I trust you? Explaining the predictions of any classifier," KDD, 2016.
  20. S. M. Lundberg and S.-I. Lee, "A unified approach to interpreting model predictions," NeurIPS, 2017.
  21. J. Adebayo et al., "Sanity checks for saliency maps," NeurIPS, 2018.
  22. F. Doshi-Velez and B. Kim, "Towards a rigorous science of interpretable machine learning," arXiv, 2017.
  23. C. Molnar, Interpretable Machine Learning, 2nd ed., 2022.
  24. Z. C. Lipton, "The mythos of model interpretability," ACM Queue, 2018.
  25. A. Arrieta et al., "Explainable artificial intelligence (XAI): Concepts, taxonomies, opportunities and challenges," Information Fusion, 2020.
  26. D. Gunning, "Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI)," DARPA Program Update, 2017.
  27. X. Shi et al., "Convolutional LSTM network: A machine learning approach for precipitation nowcasting," NeurIPS, 2015.
  28. S. Ravuri et al., "Skilful precipitation nowcasting using deep generative models of radar," Nature, 2021.
  29. S. Sonderby et al., "MetNet: A neural weather model for precipitation forecasting," arXiv, 2020.
  30. S. Andrychowicz et al., "MetNet-2: Deep learning for 12-hour precipitation forecasting," arXiv, 2021.
  31. M. Ayzel, T. Scheffer, and M. Heistermann, "RainNet v1.0: A convolutional neural network for radar-based precipitation nowcasting," Geosci. Model Dev., 2020.
  32. J. Pathak et al., "FourCastNet: A global data-driven high-resolution weather model," arXiv, 2022.
  33. K. Bi et al., "Pangu-Weather: A 3D high-resolution model for fast weather forecasting," Nature, 2023.
  34. R. Lam et al., "GraphCast: Learning skillful medium-range global weather forecasting," Science, 2023.
  35. J. A. Weyn, D. R. Durran, and R. Caruana, "Can machines learn to predict weather? Using deep learning to predict gridded 500-hPa geopotential height from historical weather data," JAMES, 2020.
  36. P. D. Dueben and P. Bauer, "Challenges and design choices for global weather and climate models based on machine learning," Geosci. Model Dev., 2018.
  37. D. Rolnick et al., "Tackling climate change with machine learning," ACM Computing Surveys, 2022.
  38. M. Reichstein et al., "Deep learning and process understanding for data-driven Earth system science," Nature, 2019.
  39. IPCC, "Climate Change 2021: The Physical Science Basis (AR6 WG1)," Cambridge Univ. Press, 2021.
  40. World Meteorological Organization, "State of the Global Climate 2023," WMO, 2024.
  41. FAO, "Climate-Smart Agriculture Sourcebook," Food and Agriculture Organization, 2013.
  42. United Nations, "Transforming our world: The 2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development," 2015.
  43. Copernicus Climate Change Service, "ERA5 reanalysis documentation," ECMWF, 2020.
  44. H. Hersbach et al., "The ERA5 global reanalysis," QJRMS, 2020.
  45. NOAA, "GOES-R Series Product Definition and Users Guide," NOAA NESDIS.
  46. T. Fawcett, "An introduction to ROC analysis," Pattern Recognition Letters, 2006.
  47. T. Saito and M. Rehmsmeier, "The precision-recall plot is more informative than the ROC plot for imbalanced datasets," PLOS ONE, 2015.
  48. D. M. W. Powers, "Evaluation: From precision, recall and F-measure to ROC, informedness and correlation," JMLT, 2011.
  49. D. Chicco and G. Jurman, "The advantages of the Matthews correlation coefficient (MCC) over F1 score and accuracy in binary classification evaluation," BMC Genomics, 2020.
  50. J. Pearl, Causality: Models, Reasoning, and Inference, 2nd ed., Cambridge Univ. Press, 2009.
  51. J. von Neumann and O. Morgenstern, Theory of Games and Economic Behavior, Princeton Univ. Press, 1944.
